topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy flyer lay crumpled on my desk, the ink stark against the white paper: "Moving to Seattle! Adventure awaits!" My hand trembled slightly as I smoothed it out, the edges crinkling under my touch. Liam. My Liam. He was moving across the country. The sheer scale of the distance hit me like a physical blow. I stared at the address, the familiar comfort of our shared apartment suddenly feeling brittle. I picked up my phone, my thumb hovering over his contact information, a strange knot tightening in my chest. I needed to call him, but the words felt lodged somewhere behind my teeth, clumsy and inadequate. I quickly shoved the flyer into a drawer, the sound of the drawer closing echoing too loudly in the quiet room.

[story 2]

I kept catching myself staring at the framed photo on my bedside table—us, laughing on a ridiculous beach trip. Now, that memory felt like a relic from another lifetime. When I saw the shipping confirmation email pop up on my screen, the color seemed to drain from the room. My shoulders instinctively hunched up around my ears, a defensive posture I hadn't realized I was adopting. It wasn't just that he was leaving; it was the sudden, sharp awareness of how much my orbit had just been disrupted. I walked to the window, watching the streetlights blur, wishing the glass would just swallow me whole. Every small movement felt oversized, clumsy under the weight of this impending separation.

[story 3]

"Seattle." The word repeated in my head, hollow and sharp. I paced the length of the living room three times, my footsteps muffled by the thick rug. I picked up a stray mug, turning it over and over in my hands until the ceramic felt warm and grounding. It was like trying to anchor myself to something solid when the ground beneath me felt like it was shifting sand. I avoided looking at my phone, afraid of what I might see, afraid of the inevitable cascade of questions and awkward goodbyes. I just wanted the silence to be permanent, a blank space where the sudden, gaping hole of his absence couldn't be felt.
